sentences.text: I was born in Warsaw, Poland, March 21, 1921, to Aaron and [?
Rahul ?] Kamm.
They were my parents.
I grew up very close family.
Because we lived in a-- in a building together with my great-grandfather, and my grandparents, uncles, and aunts.
And the building used to belong to my great-grandfather.
Then, when I was--
I would say--
Oh, immediate family.
By the end of-- by the end-- when the war started, with Germany--
We were five children.
We were five children
Five boys.
The names was-- a younger, next to me, was Paul.
The third was Saul.
The fourth one was Henrich.
And the fifth was Simon.
And when I was seven years old, I had to attend public school, which I attended for-- for a few years.
Then they-- then I went to a Jewish high school, which I stayed there till I was about 17 years old.
When the war broke out--
I-- yeah, I was very involved in sports.
I used to love to run.
Every Saturday-- there were three bridges in Warsaw, so we used to run every Saturday about 10 kilometers over the three bridges.
And I also played soccer almost every day, in school and also on a playground-- special play-- playground.
Not really.
Not really.
Not really.
We were brought up different-- separately from the Polish people.
Because when I went to public school, there were maybe 500, 600 children.
We were only two Jews.
Warsaw.
There were only two Jews-- a guy by the name of [?
Gura ?] and me.
We had to fight everyday.
Because the Polish kids used to insult us everyday, call us dirty Jew, and filthy Jew, and all kind of things.
So and I was a fighter.
I used to fight everyday.
When my younger brother and me was a little bit bigger, and he joined the school, I had a good partner with me to fight.
So this was the life in Poland.
Yeah, I went to a Jewish school.
It was very far away from my place, where we used to live.
But I used to walk everyday, about-- almost close to an hour there to the school.
And in 1939--
My father had, with a Polish man, a partnership.
They had two trucks.
And they used to haul electrical supplies from Warsaw to [?
Katowice, ?] in Poland.
And from there, they used to haul bulbs-- for light bulbs.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Made a living.
My mother-- my mother, by profession, was a butcher.
My mother, and her mother, and my aunt-- my mother's sister-- ran a kosher butcher shop in the old city, in Warsaw-- [POLISH].
And that's what she spent most of the time, before-- between children, having children.
And made-- made a-- made them all-- they made a living there.
Because-- and I was a young boy, I used to deliver meat to customers-- five o'clock in the morning, six o'clock in the morning.
And this was a--
Pardon me?
Yeah, you had to.
You had to.
I didn't have-- my mother lost her-- her father in 1920, by accident.
He was walking in the old city, [POLISH], and a roof from a building came down, and hit him, and killed him.
So I was named after him.
And I have a cousin in America, who also-- my mother's brother's son-- also named after my grandfather.
But I have to go back to start, in 1939.
Oh, this I can-- my grandfather, my father's father, was in the wholesale meat business.
He used to go out in-- in Poland, to the little towns, little villages, and buy calves-- young calves-- for slaughter, for meat, for make veal.
So he used to bring into Warsaw about between 300 and 400 veal--
Calves to slaughter, every week.
And I remember, he never took money with him.
So my grandmother used to send me, when he bought something-- send me to the main railroad station, to send the money to my grandfather, he should pay for the stuff what he bought.
The other-- most of my family were in the butcher business.
They had retail stores-- a butcher shop.
There was a-- a street in-- in-- in the place where we lived, [POLISH].
Most of the stores used to belong to our family.
My great-grandfather had a beautiful, big apartment house with 72 apartments-- apartments.
And it was burned down during the war, after the Nazis bombed Warsaw.
It was burned down.
And--
Pardon me?
I was very close with my great-grandfather.
Because I was the first great-grandchild.
So he used to love me.
I used to go around with him, every first of the month.
I used to-- he collected rent.
And I used to help him, because he was an old man.
He died when he was 96 years old.
He originally is not-- was not from Warsaw, which I found out the last few years.
Now, he was from [POLISH], in Poland, originally.
So he settled, from-- came to Warsaw many, many years ago.
Yeah, pretty much-- pretty much.
My family was very much involved in Jewish cultures.
My grand-- great-grandfather used to support a little shul, a little synagogue, in the neighborhood.
He used to pay for everything.
He used to do all kind of-- and my grandmother was the president of [NON-ENGLISH].
This is-- people used to borrow money, who didn't have any money, without interest-- so help the poor people to make a living for themselves.
Yes, yes, yes, yes.
And my fath-- my grandfather used to belong to the [?
Chevra ?] [?
Kadisha. ?] That's where he died, in 1962.
He was on the way to a meeting, to the [?
Chevra ?] [?
Kadisha. ?] And he-- he was sitting in a [NON-ENGLISH].
And he got a heart attack.
And he dropped dead.
I'm sorry. 1962.
And--
Life went on.
Life went on, till, in 1939, I was standing in front of the building.
And all of the sudden, I hear hundreds and hundreds of airplanes.
And they start bombing.
For ten hours, they bombed Warsaw-- the first day and the second day.
Group came in, group went out, group-- over 100,000 people were killed in Warsaw in the first few days, when the Germans bombed Warsaw.
About the 15th of September came an order for men, age of 16 and 55, to register for the army.
Because they said, if you go-- you have to go east.
Because they're going to form a new-- a new army, a Polish army.
So I had to go register.
And my father wasn't home then.
I registered through the army.
And they sent me-- they didn't have uniforms.
They didn't have guns-- nothing.
So they sent me to watch over a warehouse, with other people.
So I stood there.
We stood there day and night, watching nobody should steal anything.
And then, in-- in a-- a few days later, the Germans came near Warsaw.
And they bombed.
Artillery was hitting Warsaw.
And the whole thing fell apart.
The army fell apart.
Everything fell apart.
In four weeks, the war was over.
And the Germans occupied Warsaw.
The Germans occupied Warsaw.
And the first thing, they start-- and a few weeks later, they start to get-- to ask the Jewish community for people, for Jews, to go to work for the Germans.
So they had a list of all the a-- able people, to go and to work.
So I was picked.
So some-- first, I was picked to-- with my father, to work on a German-- fixing German cars, who came from the front, who were destroy-- damaged, or stuff like this.
And we had to fix them.
Then I was-- the Germans picked me to-- they had a big warehouse with gasoline at the same park.
And I was, with other Jews, made-- they made us firemen, to watch the gasoline not to-- nobody should go there, with the light, or something.
Not to have explosions.
And one day, I was taken to work at the airport, on the Warsaw Airport, which they took apart the hangars.
There were 1,200 Jews on the job.
And they took apart the hangars.
And they had pieces of steel-- maybe 150 pounds, 120 pounds.
And we had to run on our back with the-- to the trains.
Because the steel was shipped to-- to Germany.
One day-- we used to get together with friends.
We talked about the situation.
And everybody-- and everybody was talking about-- about the Germans, and what how they-- what they going to do.
Nobody knew, nobody.
Only were rumors-- all over rumors-- that the France and England is going to join the war.
And the war going to last two months.
And that's it.
But nothing happened.
Nothing happened.
Then they start building walls around, in Warsaw, in the Jewish section-- on different street, the walls.
So rumors were they built anti-- anti-tank walls.
Nobody knew what was going on.
Later on, we found out that this was-- they building the ghetto in Warsaw.
But we are-- I had a lot of friends.
We were-- we were discussing this.
And one day, I was walking on the street.
I had to wear the armband, with the star of David.
And a German came over to me, a German soldier.
And he told me-- said, come here, [GERMAN].
Jew, come here.
And he took me to-- to help unload a truck of sacks of sugar.
Each sack of sugar weighs between 180-200 pounds-- almost 100 kilos.
And I was carrying, with the other guys, the sugar.
And then of them-- in the middle, one sack of sugar fell off my back, and split, and the whole sugar this.
And he came over and start hitting me, and kicked me in my-- in my tailbone.
And I still have pains today.
So we got-- when we get together with our friends, I said, this is horrible.
Because if I would-- he wouldn't be a German, I would just squeeze him.
I was so strong.
I had an aunt in New-- in Warsaw.
She was married to a colonel, Polish colonel.
He was very visib-- very visible in the Polish society.
And her son had a printing shop.
And I used to go there.
And she used to help us.
Kuballah ?] And she used to help us with food and all kind of things.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Hm?
She was Aryan.
On the Aryan.
Yeah, she's Aryan.
And then a order came so the-- to the former army officers of the Polish Army, to register.
So what they did-- what-- if you registered, they arrested you.
And they sent you to camps.
So the Polish officers didn't want to go to camps.
So they came out.
They used to give them-- there was an organization, underground-- used to give them-- used to give them false documents.
[DRINKS]
And one day, I came to my aunt's house.
And she said to me, if I could do her a favor.
She had containers from pickles, you know?
Yeah.
And bring it to somebody-- give me an address to bring it to somebody.
In-- like a barrel.
So then I found out that her son used to make-- print these false documents for the--
For the officers, who didn't want to register.
But I didn't know that.
If the Germans would have caught me, they would kill me.
They shoot me right away.
There was no pickle in-- in the barrels.
No, there were documents.
I didn't know.
Then she said to me that she heard rumors that-- this was end of 1940.
And the ghetto was already open.
And the ghetto was closed, I think, in November, 1940.
And she said to me, there are rumors that some partisans forming near Lublin, near the city of [POLISH].
So I told-- told my friends about that one evening.
And war going to-- the rumors are the war going to last maybe two months, three months.
So let's go there.
And we're going to come back.
So we took-- bought tickets on a-- also, undercover.
Because we couldn't show.
Because we had to wear the-- Band
--band.
Yeah, sure.
And we bought tickets on the-- on a streamer going to Jozefow, near Lublin.
So we-- we left Warsaw, I think 10 or 11 friends.
When we came to Jozefow, about five friends split right away.
They went their way some place.
I don't know where they-- what happened to them.
I don't know if they're alive.
We-- we stayed in the city of Jozefow.
We didn't-- couldn't find out where the partisan is.
So after a few weeks, we found out that the partisans are forming in-- in-- in a village, [POLISH], also near-- not far from Lublin.
So we went there.
We went there.
And we found a man with a little beard.
He was the commandant of the-- of the group.
The group's name was [POLISH].
His-- the man's name was [?
Prokhovnik ?] [?
Jagosz ?] [?
Corchinsky ?].
So he told us this story, that he was a fighter in-- in Spain, against Franco, in 1936.
And now he's forming a partisan group.
When we got the partis-- when we went to the partisan-- That were Poles, not Jewish.
No, they were non-Jews.
Poles.
There were about five of them.
And we came in also, we are five Jews.
We had no guns.
We had no money, nothing.
So the first thing, he told us we have to get guns.
So he found out, through some people in the-- in the village-- we used to live-- we used to stay in the village, with different farmers, in-- how do you say [POLISH]?
Silo-- like silos.
We used to stay there, under the-- the straw.
Barn-- on the barn.
So one day, they found that one farmer has a gun.
So I went to the farmer to get-- to buy the gun from him.
It was a gun from the 1860 war, a Russian-- a Russian gun, with three bullets only.
So-- but this good.
So we decided that we have to ambush Polish police.
So everyday, we used to go on the-- and the Polish police used to ride a-- on bicycles.
That time, yes and no.
We didn't know that.
There were Polish police-- same police, like it was before the war-- the same policemen.
And so we used to ambush the policemen, take away the guns, and let them go.
So little by little, we accumulated some guns.
And we-- we didn't do anything.
We didn't bother the Germans.
The Germans didn't bother us.
Because we were, I mean, very weak.
We didn't have any-- anything.
We used to go to farmers and get food-- Ask them for bread, ask them for all kind--
That's how we survived.
But we stayed in a place.
One day-- and then-- then I'll go to further-- in June 22nd-- June 22nd, Germany attacked Russia.
And we saw already what-- when the German Army-- all over.
All the villages was full of Germans.
And we are lucky they didn't uncover us.
They attacked Russia.
And they took thousands and thousands of prisoners.
And it didn't take long.
When the Russian prisoners start running away from the camps, and they wound up in the forest, and just happened they came in the forest, where they met us, our group.
And it was good for us, because we needed more people.
We didn't have guns.
But we needed more people.
So then we got guns from the police.
And we bought some guns from the Polish population.
One day, 16-- and then already, the [NON-ENGLISH] was open, in 1941.
Concentration camp-- [NON-ENGLISH] was open.
And there were Jews who run away from there. 16 Jews came to the forest.
And they look-- they were look-- they knew that partisans are around there.
So they came and-- to us.

And they brought in with them-- because what they did, when the Jewish transports came to [NON-ENGLISH], they took all the clothing from the Jews, and went through looking for diamonds, for money, for gold.
And they had-- put everything--
And they-- eh?
No them-- for the Germans.
But also, they put in their pockets a lot.
So they came in with some money.
And he-- my commander found out that they have money.
At night, it was very quiet.
We never heard anything.
Then, all of a sudden, every night you hear shots-- one, two, three shots.
We didn't know what-- why.
Not far-- nearby.
And the Polack, the people we stayed with in this house, tells us that the 16 Jews-- two of them are gone.
Something happened to them.
Anyway, after two months, all 16 Jews were killed by my-- our commander.
By the partisan commander.
So we had in our group already Jews and Russians.
So we decided to make an ambush on our commander and in the-- his people, who killed the 16 Jews, to get the money from them.
And at night, we-- we caught them, but not our commander.
He run away.
And we killed all this-- 10 of his people.
And we-- a war started between the former commander and us.
He found another--
--group someplace.
And he came.
And he-- we were fighting each other.
Then more-- more Russian came to our group.
And we became a group of about a hundred.
And I was the only Jew.
The other friends were killed at the-- because of the only, the other thing.
They got drunk in a house-- somebody's house.
And they had-- the Polish men called the police.
And--
Yeah.
So-- so, yeah.
So we decided, then and there-- because it wasn't no room for us to fight them there.
So we heard that in the east, in the Polish Ukraine, they're forming big partisan groups.
